title: There Are Infinitely Many Elliptic Curves Over the Rationals of Rank 2
speaker: David Zywina
abstract: For an elliptic curve E defined over Q, the Mordell-Weil group E(Q) is a finitely generated abelian group. We prove that there are infinitely many elliptic curves E over Q for which E(Q) has rank 2. Our elliptic curves will be given by explicit models and their ranks will be found using a 2-descent. The infinitude of such elliptic curves will make use of a theorem of Tao and Ziegler. Time permitting we also describe some recent work on rank stability.

proof_stages.label: Rank question and lower-bound specialization
proof_stages.summary: After recalling Mordell's theorem and the question of which ranks occur infinitely often, the speaker explains that elliptic surfaces over Q(T) with independent sections give many rational specializations of rank at least two by Silverman's specialization theorem.

proof_stages.label: Selmer obstruction to proving exact rank
proof_stages.summary: To prove rank exactly two one needs upper bounds from computable Selmer groups, but in a typical family the local conditions require knowing the bad primes, which amounts to factoring a varying irreducible discriminant.

proof_stages.label: Controlled rank-two surface with linear discriminant
proof_stages.summary: He introduces a specially searched non-isotrivial elliptic curve over Q(T) with rational 2-torsion, geometric rank two, two independent points, and discriminant a product of linear factors, then normalizes specializations T=m/n^2 to avoid extra bad primes.

proof_stages.label: Prime-valued specialization theorem
proof_stages.summary: The main theorem chooses coprime positive m,n for which the relevant linear discriminant factors are primes congruent to 11 mod 24; Tao-Ziegler supplies infinitely many such pairs, giving controlled bad primes and infinitely many distinct j-invariants.

proof_stages.label: Two-isogeny Selmer descent
proof_stages.summary: Using the rational 2-torsion, he passes to a degree-2 isogeny, realizes the associated Selmer groups as square classes whose covering curves are locally soluble everywhere, and computes both the isogeny and dual Selmer groups by explicit local conditions.

proof_stages.label: Equality with known points and rank-two conclusion
proof_stages.summary: The computed Selmer groups are generated by the images of the known two independent sections together with the 2-torsion point, so the weak Mordell-Weil group is as small as possible and the specialized curves have rank exactly two.

proof_stages.label: Rank-stability problem over quadratic extensions
proof_stages.summary: In the Hilbert-tenth-problem setting, he recalls rank-stability results for abelian varieties over quadratic extensions and states his in-progress strengthening: for any quadratic extension L/K there should be an elliptic curve over K whose rank stays one over L.

proof_stages.label: Number-field prime construction and Selmer balancing
proof_stages.summary: For the rank-one strengthening he uses a non-isotrivial rank-one family with linear discriminant, a number-field prime-values theorem to choose parameters satisfying many local conditions, and a product-formula balancing of the two 2-isogeny Selmer groups so that all F2-linear local relations are used.

examples.name: Warm-up rank-two surface with irreducible discriminant
examples.note: The deliberately ordinary rank-two family over Q(T) shows that Silverman specialization gives rank at least two but an irreducible discriminant leaves bad primes uncontrollable, motivating the later search for a linear-discriminant family.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker uses the rank-two function-field surface to specialize t, invoke Silverman injectivity, examine its irreducible discriminant, and explain why the resulting bad-prime/Selmer computation becomes inaccessible.

passes.reason: An otherwise ordinary rank-two elliptic surface is used as an enemy case for the naive specialization strategy because its irreducible discriminant makes bad primes and Selmer upper bounds uncontrollable.

An otherwise ordinary rank-2 elliptic surface is used to show the naive specialization approach runs into inaccessible Selmer computations from irreducible discriminants.
An otherwise ordinary rank-two elliptic surface with irreducible discriminant is used as a foil showing that naive specialization gives lower bounds but leaves Selmer upper bounds inaccessible.

examples.name: Root-number check for the 11 mod 24 congruence
examples.note: He notes that, although not used in the proof, local root-number computations explain heuristically why the chosen congruence is a good search region and why nearby choices lead to parity or Sha difficulties.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker explicitly describes computing the local root-number factors, multiplying them so cancellations occur, and checking that the 11 mod 24 congruence makes the resulting root number equal to one.

passes.reason: The 11 mod 24 congruence class is a standard arithmetic-progression condition chosen to make the root-number sanity check come out correctly.
The 11 mod 24 congruence condition is an ordinary residue-class choice used as a positive root-number sanity check, not to refute a claim.
The residue condition 11 mod 24 is an ordinary congruence class chosen to make the root number come out +1 as a consistency check.
The 11 mod 24 congruence is an ordinary arithmetic restriction used as a consistency check making the root number compatible with rank 2.
The 11 mod 24 congruence is an ordinary residue condition used as a supportive root-number sanity check, not to refute a claim.
the 11 mod 24 prime congruence is a systematic residue-class condition used as a parity sanity check
An ordinary congruence condition used positively as a root-number sanity check rather than to refute a claim.
The 11 mod 24 congruence is a chosen ordinary residue condition used as a positive root-number sanity check, not to refute anything.
ordinary congruence condition used as a sanity check ensuring root number +1 rather than as a disproof or structural exception
The 11 mod 24 condition is used as a consistency/root-number check selecting the expected even-parity case, not as a structurally special object.
The 11 mod 24 condition is an ordinary congruence class used as a consistency check that the root number is +1.
The 11 mod 24 condition is presented as a supporting parity sanity check, not as a failure case, and is just an ordinary congruence condition.

examples.name: Hypothetical 3-descent alternative
examples.note: In response to a question about other isogenies, he contrasts the clean 2-isogeny descent with a possible 3-descent that would force computations in cubic fields and class groups, illustrating why the proof is engineered around 2-torsion.
examples.engagement_reason: The possible 3-descent route is only discussed hypothetically as something one might try, with obstacles such as cubic fields and class groups noted but no descent carried out.

passes.reason: No specific curve or family is specified, only a hypothetical 3-descent approach whose complications are mentioned.

examples.name: Audience's fixed-j twisting family
examples.note: The question starts from a fixed curve y^2=x^3+Ax+B and varies it by square and cube rational functions, letting the speaker contrast isotrivial twisting, where bad primes are controlled, with his non-isotrivial specialization method.
examples.engagement_reason: The audience’s fixed-j twisting setup is restated and compared generally with non-isotrivial families, but no concrete twisting family is constructed or computed with.
